refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. The p95 metric ensures consistent performance under realistic network variability. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning network, vehicle, and control systems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with safety manager oversight. Establish baseline metrics for p50, p95, p99 latencies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, state, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the safety system.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency network protocol (e.g., UDP-based custom protocol) with priority queuing and adaptive compression to minimize command and telemetry transmission time.
DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining standard IP networking with edge computing (local compute nodes near vehicle) to reduce round-trip distance and latency.
DP-C: Use a commercial 5G/LTE network with QoS guarantees and fallback to local WiFi mesh for redundancy; implement client-side prediction and dead-reckoning to mask latency.
DP-D: Establish a multi-path communication strategy with real-time latency monitoring, automatic failover, and operator notification of latency state changes.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Establish a multi-path communication strategy with real-time latency monitoring, automatic failover, and operator notification of latency state changes. This DP combines network resilience (multi-path), observability (latency monitoring), and safety integration (operator notification) to meet the p95 ≤ 150 ms requirement while accommodating variable network conditions and enabling graceful degradation.

potential_sources: DP-D couples to network infrastructure selection (5G, WiFi, satellite options) but does not mandate a specific technology.
DP-D couples to vehicle control architecture (must support command queuing and state reporting).
DP-D couples to safety system (latency violations must trigger SafetyAlert interface).
DP-D couples to operator interface (must display latency state and network health).
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because it reflects genuine system dependencies. DP-D is technology-agnostic at the strategy level, allowing flexibility in network selection while enforcing latency observability and safety integration.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure (5G, WiFi, satellite) can be provisioned to support p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed conditions.
Assumption: Vehicle control systems can process commands and generate telemetry within 50 ms.
Risk: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed parameters, p95 target may not be achievable; mitigation requires operator training and safety procedures for latency-degraded operation.
Risk: Multi-path failover logic may introduce transient latency spikes during switchover; mitigation requires careful state synchronization and operator notification.
Risk: Latency monitoring overhead itself may consume bandwidth or compute resources; mitigation requires efficient monitoring implementation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound. Recommend adding explicit sub-DPs for: (1) latency measurement methodology (e.g., timestamping at command origin, vehicle receipt, telemetry return), (2) failover decision criteria (e.g., latency threshold, packet loss threshold), and (3) operator notification protocol (e.g., visual/audio alerts, latency display refresh rate).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency requirement. It encompasses command transmission, telemetry reception, latency monitoring, multi-path failover logic, and operator notification. It interfaces with Vehicle (for command delivery and telemetry ingestion), Safety Services (for latency violation alerts), and external Network Link (for multi-path communication).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator interface. Tasking is the input channel for remote control actions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and video feed to tele-operator. Telemetry is the output channel for situational awareness. Latency of this interface is critical to the FR.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports latency violations, network degradation, or communication failures to Safety Services. Enables safety system to enforce operational constraints or trigger emergency procedures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network infrastructure (5G, WiFi, satellite, etc.). Teleoperation module uses Network Link to transmit Tasking and receive Telemetry. Multi-path failover logic operates at this interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own teleoperation latency as a specific responsibility. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership across multiple sub-systems and obscure accountability. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns command processing and telemetry generation latency (sub-FRs 008.2 and 008.3) but does not own network transmission latency (sub-FR 008.1 and 008.3 network portion) or latency monitoring/failover logic. Assigning the full FR to Vehicle would conflate vehicle control with network management. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation and naturally owns end-to-end latency management, multi-path failover, latency monitoring, and operator notification. This is the primary responsibility of the Teleoperation module. High containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety-critical response to latency violations (e.g., emergency stop, mode constraints) but does not own the latency measurement or failover logic itself. Assigning the full FR to Safety Services would conflate safety response with latency management. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008. It has clear responsibility for command transmission, telemetry reception, latency monitoring, and multi-path failover. It interfaces with Vehicle (for command/telemetry exchange), Safety Services (for latency violation alerts), and Network Link (for multi-path communication). Ownership is unambiguous and diffusion is minimal.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle control latency (sub-FR 008.2) exceeds 50 ms, the Teleoperation module cannot compensate. Mitigation: Establish clear latency budget allocation between network (75 ms) and vehicle (50 ms) and enforce via separate sub-FR ownership.

Risk: If Network Link interface is not well-defined or multi-path failover logic is not standardized, Teleoperation module may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies. Mitigation: Define Network Link as an abstraction layer with clear SLAs (latency, availability, failover time).

Risk: Latency monitoring and failover logic may require real-time processing; if Teleoperation module is implemented as a software service on a shared compute platform, resource contention may degrade latency. Mitigation: Allocate dedicated compute resources or implement priority scheduling for Teleoperation module.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module. Recommend establishing a clear latency budget: Network (75 ms p95), Vehicle (50 ms p95), with 25 ms margin for system integration. Define Network Link interface SLAs explicitly. Establish ownership of sub-FRs: Teleoperation owns 008.1, 008.3 (network portion), and 008.4; Vehicle owns 008.2 and 008.3 (vehicle portion).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-path communication strategy with latency monitoring and failover) directly addresses the FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP-D is technology-agnostic, allowing flexibility in network selection while enforcing observability and safety integration. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified. Coupling to network infrastructure and vehicle control is acceptable and reflects genuine system dependencies.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module implementation is distributed across multiple code repositories or physical nodes, latency monitoring and failover logic may become fragmented. Mitigation: Establish Teleoperation as a cohesive module with clear boundaries and centralized latency management logic.

Risk: If Network Link abstraction is not enforced, Teleoperation module may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies (5G, WiFi, etc.). Mitigation: Define Network Link as a formal interface with version control and SLA enforcement.
Risk: If Safety Services integration is ad-hoc, SafetyAlert notifications may be delayed or missed. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert as a priority interface with guaranteed delivery and low latency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for the full FR. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and necessary. No artificial diffusion detected. Ownership is clear and accountability is unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-008 is feasible via latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Recommend establishing a test harness that measures round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) and reports p50, p95, p99 percentiles. Operator acceptance testing with safety manager oversight is required.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-008 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FR allocation is also clear: Teleoperation owns network latency (008.1, 008.3 network portion, 008.4); Vehicle owns command processing and telemetry generation latency (008.2, 008.3 vehicle portion). Latency budget allocation (Network 75 ms, Vehicle 50 ms) is explicit and measurable.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit latency budget: Network (75 ms p95), Vehicle (50 ms p95), with 25 ms margin for system integration.
Define Network Link interface SLAs: latency, availability, failover time, packet loss tolerance.
Establish sub-FR ownership: Teleoperation owns 008.1, 008.3 (network portion), 008.4; Vehicle owns 008.2, 008.3 (vehicle portion).
Define latency measurement methodology: timestamping at command origin, vehicle receipt, telemetry return; p95 calculation method.
Establish SafetyAlert protocol: latency violation thresholds, notification timing, operator response procedures.
Conduct operator acceptance testing with safety manager oversight to validate p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (008.1, 008.2, 008.3, 008.4) that address distinct aspects of the latency requirement: network transmission, vehicle processing, telemetry transmission, and latency monitoring/safety integration. Each sub-FR requires its own FR-DP-IM decomposition to establish clear ownership, interfaces, and verification criteria. Proceeding to the next level will clarify latency budget allocation, interface definitions, and implementation responsibilities.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, state, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the safety system.

assumptions: Network infrastructure (5G, WiFi, satellite) can be provisioned to support p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed conditions.
Vehicle control systems can process commands and generate telemetry within 50 ms.
Latency budget allocation: Network 75 ms, Vehicle 50 ms, System margin 25 ms.
Operator training and safety procedures are in place for latency-degraded operation.
Multi-path failover logic can be implemented without introducing unacceptable latency spikes.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, making p95 target unachievable.
Multi-path failover logic may introduce transient latency spikes during switchover.
Latency monitoring overhead may consume bandwidth or compute resources.
Vehicle control latency may exceed 50 ms budget if not carefully designed.
Teleoperation module implementation may become fragmented across multiple code repositories or physical nodes.
evidence_needed: Network latency baseline measurements under agreed conditions (5G, WiFi, satellite options).
Vehicle control latency measurements (command processing, telemetry generation).
Latency monitoring implementation design and overhead analysis.
Multi-path failover logic design and state synchronization strategy.
Operator acceptance testing results with safety manager oversight.
Teleoperation module architecture and interface definitions.
SafetyAlert protocol and latency violation response procedures.
